46,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_25_p085_f13f7dbe,"Selling antiquities, ancient and ethnographic art online since 1993, Artemis Gallery specializes in Classical Antiquities (Egyptian, Greek, Roman, Near Eastern), Asian, Pre-Columbian, African / Tribal / Oceanographic art. Each stands slightly forward on its realistic hoof with two claws displayed, the cuboid slightly set over the back of the hoof rather than directly over it, with two naturalistic projections from the back, showing that the artisans who sculpted the molds to create these feet paid close attention to the details of bull anatomy. Four matching, more-than-life-sized cast bronze bull hooves, each capped by a large, cuboid shape, and with two have projecting slots atop the cuboid for attachment. The cuboid projections and slots on two of them suggest that they were made to hold a table or altar rather than a full statue of a bull. Another possibility is that these feet were part of a naos - a simple rectangular temple form - as in an example found near Haoulti, which had four feet shaped like bull's hooves, two pointing forward, and two pointing back, supporting a base that held a niche decorated with ornamentation. Size of each (all are relatively similar): 5.75"" W x 11"" H (14.6 cm x 27.9 cm) What might these massive hooves have graced? 1st millennium BCE to early 1st millennium CE, probably later in the period based on the lead weight, which was a later technological innovation to stabilize monumental statuary. Out of stock Description Additional information Ancient South Arabia is a geographical label referring to a region occupied by six semitic kingdoms: Sabaʼ, Qatabān, Ma‘īn, Ḥaḍramawt, the Kingdom of Awsan, and the Himyarite Kingdom. The territory of these kingdoms corresponds to a modern-day area including Yemen, and extending into Oman, north to the Arabian oasis of Dedan, to Ethiopia, and even as far along as the East African Coast, into modern Tanzania. Their depiction is thus endowed with significance in several contexts: in religious rituals, as mythical creatures, and as incarnations or symbols of gods and goddesses. Animals occupied a prominent place in ancient art across a number of civilisations and across a variety of media, including painting, pottery, and jewellery.",105,695.00,GBP,"Figurine, alabaster: a realistically carved South Arabian camel, depicted as a dromedary standing four-square with head erect.","Figurine, alabaster: a realistically carved South Arabian camel, identified as a dromedary, standing four-square with its head erect. The object is described as belonging to Ancient South Arabia, a geographical label for a region occupied by the kingdoms of Sabaʼ, Qatabān, Ma‘īn, Ḥaḍramawt, the Kingdom of Awsan, and the Himyarite Kingdom. The source notes that the territory of these kingdoms corresponds to parts of modern-day Yemen and Oman, extending north to the Arabian oasis of Dedan, to Ethiopia, and as far as the East African coast, including modern Tanzania. The text also states that animal depictions in this region had significance in religious rituals and as mythical creatures, as well as as incarnations or symbols of gods and goddesses.",57;;;;;;;

column1: 63,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_10_p063_7c650d5d,"LOT 64228291 South Arabian Bronze Statuette of a Rider [ translate ] A South Arabian hollow-casted bronze statuette of a male rider, portrayed in a stylised manner. A triangular opening features on his chest and back, whilst a belt is wrapped around his waist, a hint to his clothing together with short vertical grooves on his straight legs. The territory of these kingdoms corresponds to a modern-day area including Yemen, and extending into Oman, north to the Arabian oasis of Dedan, to Ethiopia, and even as far along as the East African Coast, into modern Tanzania. Ancient South Arabia is a geographical label referring to a region occupied by six Semitic kingdoms: Sabaʼ, Qatabān, Ma‘īn, Ḥaḍramawt, the Kingdom of Awsan, and the Himyarite Kingdom. 5cm (the measurements provided are not inclusive of the stand) . 78,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_10_p080_6756332a,"LOT 0164 A RASULID LACQUERED CYLINDIRCAL WOOD BOX, EGYPT OR YEMEN, 14TH CENTURY [ translate ] With flat base and slightly sloping sides, the upper edge with slight recess to fit a lid, the exterior wooden surface overlaid in a succession of black, red, yellow and green lacquers, then carved through to reveal a polychrome design, the broad central band with lobed rectangular panels containing scrolling arabesques around quatrefoil roundels, the borders of the panels entwining and continuing into lobed concentric quatrefoils dividing each panel, a band below of stylized thuluth calligraphy interrupted by roundels containing quatrefoil panels, the base and interior plain with remains of monochrome lacquer, 16.2 cm. The technique of decoration of our box is in the same tradition ans would point to an attribution to 14th or 15th century Mamluk Egypt indicated by the finds from Qusair al-Qadim.â€¨â€¨The shape of our object has counterparts in other items from Mamluk Egypt. diam.PROVENANCE Christieâ€™s, 17th October 1995, lot 269.Private collection, GreeceCATALOGUE NOTE While later Islamic lacquer has been studied extensively, the history of the technique in early Islamic times is still obscure. Given the possible Chinese origins of the technique it is certainly relevant that there exist a number of 15th century cylindrical lidded boxes from China, decorated in a Chinese manner with peony scrolls and phoenixes (Garner, Sir H.: Chinese lacquer, London, 1979, figs. In Persia a small fragment was found during the excavations at Ghubayra which has been given a thirteenth century attribution (Fehervari, G., â€˜Near Eastern Lacquerwork",161,,,"Box, wood, cylindrical, with flat base and slightly sloping sides, 14th century, Egypt or Yemen. The exterior is overlaid with black, red, yellow, and green lacquers and carved through to form a polychrome decorative design with scrolling arabesques, quatrefoil roundels, lobed rectangular panels, and stylized thuluth calligraphy.","Box, wood, cylindrical, with flat base and slightly sloping sides and a slight recess at the upper edge to receive a lid, 14th century, Egypt or Yemen. The exterior wooden surface is overlaid in successive layers of black, red, yellow, and green lacquer and carved through to reveal a polychrome decoration. The broad central band is composed of lobed rectangular panels containing scrolling arabesques around quatrefoil roundels, with panel borders that entwine and continue into lobed concentric quatrefoils dividing each panel. 104,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_5_p009_16162ad0,"Brazier of Rasulid Sultan al-Malik al-Muzaffar Shams al-Din Yusuf ibn 'Umar second half 13th century On view at The Met Fifth Avenue in Gallery 454 Braziers served as portable grills and heaters. The lionheaded knobs with rings provided receptacles for handles to transport the heated unit, while the confronted dragon heads on each side functioned as spit brackets. The names and honorifics mentioned in the monumental inscription identify the patron, the second ruler of the Rasulid dynasty (r. 1250–95). His dynastic emblem, a five‑petalled rosette upon a circular shield, features prominently on both sides of each corner bracket. Artwork Details Object Information Overview Catalogue Entry Signatures, Inscriptions, and Markings Provenance Exhibition History References Title: Brazier of Rasulid Sultan al-Malik al-Muzaffar Shams al-Din Yusuf ibn 'Umar Date: second half 13th century Geography: Probably made in Egypt Medium: Brass",268,,,"Brazier, brass, second half 13th century. Probably made in Egypt. The object is associated with Rasulid Sultan al-Malik al-Muzaffar Shams al-Din Yusuf ibn 'Umar and bears a monumental inscription naming the patron.","Brazier, brass, second half 13th century. Probably made in Egypt. This portable brazier was used as a grill and heater. It has lion-headed knobs with rings that served as receptacles for handles to transport the heated unit, and confronted dragon heads on each side that functioned as spit brackets. The monumental inscription names and honors the patron, identified as the second ruler of the Rasulid dynasty, al-Malik al-Muzaffar Shams al-Din Yusuf ibn 'Umar (r. 1250–95). His dynastic emblem, a five-petalled rosette on a circular shield, appears prominently on both sides of each corner bracket.",105;;;;;;;

Rautenburghs Trompe l'œil with letters Estimate: 6,000 - 8,000 GBP Italian, Sienese, 15th century Figure of the Virgin Estimate: 5,000 - 7,000 USD Bolognese School, early 17th century Portrait of a boy, bust-length, in a white ruff Estimate: 8,000 - 12,000 GBP Charles Théodore Frère Encampment in the desert Estimate: 40,000 - 60,000 GBP Charles James Theriat The Caravan Estimate: 30,000 - 50,000 GBP Nasreddine (Etienne) Dinet A Captive Audience Estimate: 50,000 - 70,000 GBP Originally from the storied collection of Christiane and Antonin Besse who lovingly built a collection of South Arabian sculpture while living in the then British colony of Aden in the middle 20 th century (see ), this sculpture stands as a compelling testament to the religious life, social structure, and artistic sophistication of ancient South Arabia, offering rare insight into a culture whose visual traditions remain less familiar than those of its contemporaries, yet equally rich and expressive. This lot has been withdrawn Lot Details Description A South Arabian Alabaster Figure of a Woman, Qataban 3rd Century B.C./1st Century A.D. Origins II Origins II A South Arabian Alabaster Figure of a Woman, Qataban, 3rd Century B.C./1st Century A.D. Carved from translucent calcite-alabaster, a material prized for its luminous qualities, the figure displays the stylized aesthetic characteristic of South Arabian figurative art: a frontal pose, simplified bodily forms, and an emphasis on facial presence and symmetry. Originally, such figures may have featured painted details or inlaid facial detailing, such as extant here in the figures recessed eyes, enhancing their lifelike presence and ritual impact. You May Also Like Charles Robertson The Wailing Wall Estimate: 100,000 - 150,000 GBP Stanislaus von Chlebowski A Dervish before the Mosque Estimate: 80,000 - 120,000 GBP Fabio Fabbi Portrait of Markos Botsaris, after Jean-Léon Gérôme Estimate: 60,000 - 80,000 GBP Alberto Pasini Caravan traversing a mountain pass Estimate: 80,000 - 120,000 GBP C.",355,,,"Figure of a woman, alabaster, Qataban, 3rd century B.C./1st century A.D. South Arabian sculpture carved from translucent calcite-alabaster, with a frontal pose, simplified bodily forms, and recessed eyes.","Figure of a woman, alabaster, Qataban, 3rd century B.C./1st century A.D. South Arabian sculpture carved from translucent calcite-alabaster. The figure is described as displaying the stylized aesthetic characteristic of South Arabian figurative art, with a frontal pose, simplified bodily forms, and an emphasis on facial presence and symmetry. The recessed eyes are noted as extant, and the text states that such figures may originally have featured painted details or inlaid facial detailing. The object is identified as a South Arabian alabaster figure of a woman from Qataban.",108;;;;;;;

The Greek inscription ΑΛΕΞΑΝ∆ΡΟΥ is written vertically to the right of the figure of Zeus. 169,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_28_p010_29618710,"Ultimately, this is a placeholder solution so that more time can be spent on fingerprinting and identifying headless browsers (EG: via how they do font rendering) so that the challenge proof of work page doesn't need to be presented to users that are much more likely to be legitimate. The idea is that at individual scales the additional load is ignorable, but at mass scraper levels it adds up and makes scraping much more expensive. Anubis uses a Proof-of-Work scheme in the vein of Hashcash, a proposed proof-of-work scheme for reducing email spam. Please note that Anubis requires the use of modern JavaScript features that plugins like JShelter will disable. This can and does cause downtime for the websites, which makes their resources inaccessible for everyone. 177,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_4_p003_16facb8e,"The Barakat Collection Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Sabaean Seated Statuette of a Woman , 600 BCE - 100 BCE Calcite-Alabaster height 20.3 cm height 8 in LA.550 Enquire %3Cdiv%20class%3D%22title_and_year%22%3E%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_title%22%3ESabaean%20Seated%20Statuette%20of%20a%20Woman%3C/span%3E%2C%20%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_year%22%3E600%20BCE%20%20-%20%20100%20BCE%3C/span%3E%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22medium%22%3ECalcite-Alabaster%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22dimensions%22%3Eheight%2020.3%20cm%3Cbr/%3E%0Aheight%208%20in%3C/div%3E The ancient kingdom of Saba ruled over the lands of southern Arabia, centered in modern day Yemen. In addition, a trade route that connected India to Egypt that passed through their capital of Marib was another major source of wealth.In the 1st Century A.D., the Ptolemaic Greeks discovered a sea route from India directly to the port of Alexandria, eliminating Saba from her lucrative trade and ushering in the decline of Sabean prosperity. Yet the pose of the figurine would indicate a probably southern provenance, as several early ones have been unearthed from Main, while the use of alabaster was widespread in Qataban. Although gold and silver deposits were present, the chief source of their vast wealth was derived from their veritable monopoly of two of the most coveted materials in ancient times: frankincense and myrrh, resinous gums obtained from certain trees that only grow in Southern Arabia and were literally worth their weight in gold. The female figure is seated wearing a long dress, her hands held before her, the ears and arms pierced,the lower arms projecting horizontally forward at right angles to the upper arms, in a gesture very common in such statuary. Saba is perhaps better known as Sheba, the Hebrew word for the kingdom, whose famous Queen was recounted as having visited Solomon in the pages of the Old Testament. Ruins of fortresses and walled towns are evident and remnants of their extensive irrigation system that turned the desert into a paradise still cover the land. Biblical accounts speak of the wealth of this ancient civilization of traders and merchants, and modern archaeological excavations confirm these reports.",550,,,"Sabaean seated statuette of a woman, calcite-alabaster, dated 600 BCE–100 BCE, with a height of 20.3 cm. The female figure is seated wearing a long dress, with her hands held before her.","Sabaean seated statuette of a woman, calcite-alabaster, dated 600 BCE–100 BCE, height 20.3 cm. The female figure is seated and wears a long dress;" her hands are held before her. The ears and arms are pierced

179,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_4_p008_edea912b,"The Barakat Collection Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Sabean Granite Head of a Woman , 600 BCE - 100 BCE Granite 24.1 x 10.8 cm 9 1/2 x 4 1/4 in LK.110 Enquire %3Cdiv%20class%3D%22title_and_year%22%3E%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_title%22%3ESabean%20Granite%20Head%20of%20a%20Woman%3C/span%3E%2C%20%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_year%22%3E600%20BCE%20%20-%20%20100%20BCE%3C/span%3E%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22medium%22%3EGranite%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22dimensions%22%3E24.1%20x%2010.8%20cm%3Cbr/%3E%0A9%201/2%20x%204%201/4%20in%3C/div%3E Further images (View a larger image of thumbnail 1 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 2 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 3 ) This striking head is an architectural fragment from a notable public or ceremonial building, and pertains to the ancient kingdom of Saba which ruled over the lands of southwestern Arabia,... The city of Marib was also in an ideal position to control the trade route between India to Egypt, although this lucrative venture was cut short in the 1st century AD following the foundation of a nautical route from India directly to the port of Alexandria. Read more This striking head is an architectural fragment from a notable public or ceremonial building, and pertains to the ancient kingdom of Saba which ruled over the lands of southwestern Arabia, centered in modern day Yemen. There are major works in bronze, precious metals and exotic minerals, but they are perhaps best known for works in stone such as alabaster and, in the present case, granite. The wealth of the kingdom was legendary, and was primarily attributable to Saba’s position at the crossroads of the ancient world, receiving consignments (particularly of incense) from all across the Middle East, Asia and the Mediterranean basin. Technically, the Sabeans are one of four major powers in this area, also including the Minaeans, the Qatabanians and the Hadramites, but the peoples as a whole became subsumed as a single entity by the middle of the first millennium BC. Their religion and mythology fuelled the themes of their sculptural works – primarily anthropomorphic and zoomorphic statuary – while their contact with other cultures and nations led to a highly derived and distinctive style. However, the breaks to the top, side and base of this piece suggest that it was architectural in origin, and may have served as a form of caryatid for a structure of social import to the society at the time.",560,,,"Sabean head of a woman in granite, dated 600 BCE–100 BCE, measuring 24.1 x 10.8 cm. The object is an architectural fragment from the ancient kingdom of Saba.","Sabean head of a woman, granite, dated 600 BCE–100 BCE. The object measures 24.1 x 10.8 cm (9 1/2 x 4 1/4 in). It is described as an architectural fragment from a notable public or ceremonial building in the ancient kingdom of Saba, centered in modern-day Yemen. The piece is identified as a striking head and is noted for breaks to the top, side, and base, suggesting an architectural origin. The source states that it may have served as a caryatid for a structure of social importance. The Sabeans are described as being known for stone works, including alabaster and granite, and for a distinctive sculptural style shaped by religious and cultural contacts.",180;;;;;;;

180,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_4_p009_90e33534,"The Barakat Collection Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Open a larger version of the following image in a popup: Sabean Alabaster Relief of a Horned Bull , 200 BCE - 100 CE Alabaster 10.2 x 14 cm 4 1/8 x 5 1/2 in PF.0083 Enquire %3Cdiv%20class%3D%22title_and_year%22%3E%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_title%22%3ESabean%20Alabaster%20Relief%20of%20a%20Horned%20Bull%3C/span%3E%2C%20%3Cspan%20class%3D%22title_and_year_year%22%3E200%20BCE%20%20-%20%20100%20CE%3C/span%3E%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22medium%22%3EAlabaster%3C/div%3E%3Cdiv%20class%3D%22dimensions%22%3E10.2%20x%2014%20cm%3Cbr/%3E%0A4%201/8%20x%205%201/2%20in%3C/div%3E Further images (View a larger image of thumbnail 1 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 2 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 3 ) (View a larger image of thumbnail 4 ) The ancient kingdom of Saba ruled over the lands of southern Arabia, centered in modern day Yemen. In the 1st Century A.D., the Ptolemaic Greeks discovered a sea route from India directly to the port of Alexandria, eliminating Saba from this lucrative trade and ushering in the decline of Sabean prosperity. Carved from one smooth block of alabaster, this beautiful high relief sculpture signifies an important period of art in the South Arabian region, using bold curves and delicate decoration. Although gold and silver deposits were present, the chief source of their vast wealth was derived from their veritable monopoly of two of the most coveted materials in ancient times: frankincense and myrrh, resinous gums obtained from certain trees that only grow in Southern Arabia and were literally worth their weight in gold. Perhaps their greatest accomplishment was the Great Dam of Marib, a monumental construction that brought water from the mountains both to the city and to the crop fields. The dam was in continual use (accounting for timely repairs) until the 6th Century A.D. Saba is perhaps better known as Sheba, the Hebrew word for the kingdom, whose famous Queen was recounted as having visited Solomon in the pages of the Old Testament. Ruins of fortresses and walled towns are evident and remnants of their extensive irrigation system that turned the desert into a paradise still cover the land.",561,,,"Relief, alabaster. Sabean alabaster relief of a horned bull, dated 200 BCE–100 CE. Carved from one smooth block of alabaster;" dimensions 10.2 x 14 cm (4 1/8 x 5 1/2 in).""

188,0-آثارنا المنهوبة 30_p010_82b6e20a,"Standing male figure ca. late 1st millennium BCE Not on view This standing male figure is a funerary or votive object intended to be placed in a tomb or temple. Dressed in a long, plain garment reaching almost to the ankles, the figure stands in a fully frontal pose. The base, which is carved in one piece with the figure, bears the name of the individual and his clan—Sadiqim Ma'ad. The eyes and eyebrows are carved to hold inlays, now missing, and the arms are bent at the elbows, extending out at right angles to the body. One hand is clenched in a fist, pierced vertically to hold some object, perhaps a scepter, now lost. The other hand is open. The undersurface of the base and the top and back of the figure's head are roughly carved. Such statues were often set in niches, and the surfaces not visible were consequently left unfinished on many examples. The placement of the ears high on the head, the aquiline nose, the small mouth, and the fully frontal pose are all characteristic of human images made in this region. Funerary or votive object carved in a fully frontal pose, with a long plain garment and a base inscribed with the name Sadiqim Ma'ad.","Standing male figure, alabaster (calcite), ca. late 1st millennium BCE. This funerary or votive object was intended for placement in a tomb or temple. The figure stands frontally and wears a long, plain garment reaching almost to the ankles. It is carved in one piece with a base that bears the name of the individual and his clan, Sadiqim Ma'ad. The eyes and eyebrows are carved to receive inlays, now missing. The arms are bent at the elbows and extend outward at right angles to the body;" one hand is clenched in a fist and pierced vertically to hold an object

192,0-آثارنا المنهوبة 30_p014_9bc22dd3,"Votive plaque inscribed with Sabaean dedication ca. late 1st millennium BCE Not on view This votive plaque with a raised border around its edges has a Sabaean dedicatory inscription. While the worship of aniconic images was widespread among South Arabian cultures, the precise significance of the inscription as a cult object is uncertain. The practice of dedicating the inscription itself, as opposed to placing the inscription on an object intended for dedication, is a departure from the contemporary customs of greater Mesopotamia and Iran. 195,0-آثارنا المنهوبة 30_p018_880a3521,"Incense burner ca. mid-1st millennium BCE Not on view This bronze incense burner from southwestern Arabia consists of a cylindrical cup set on a conical base. A rectangular architectural form suggesting a facade extends upward from the cup’s front. Its face is decorated with two serpents flanking a round disk set within a crescent, all in low relief. Cast separately, an ibex standing on a plinth projects from the front of the incense burner and may serve as a handle. From the middle of the first millennium B.C. until the sixth century A.D., the kingdoms of southwestern Arabia gained considerable wealth and power through their control of the trade in incense between Arabia and the lands of the Mediterranean seacoast. Frankincense and myrrh, gum resins that are native to southern Arabia, were widely valued in the ancient world for the preparation of incense, perfumes, cosmetics, and medicines, as well as for use in religious and funerary ceremonies. The importance of incense in the religion of southwestern Arabia is reflected in this object",583,,,"Incense burner, bronze, from southwestern Arabia, ca. mid-1st millennium BCE. It has a cylindrical cup on a conical base, with a rectangular architectural facade rising from the front, decorated in low relief with two serpents flanking a round disk within a crescent. A separately cast ibex on a plinth projects from the front and may have served as a handle.","Incense burner, bronze, from southwestern Arabia, dated to ca. mid-1st millennium BCE. The vessel consists of a cylindrical cup set on a conical base, with a rectangular architectural form rising from the front of the cup and suggesting a facade. The facade is decorated in low relief with two serpents flanking a round disk set within a crescent. A separately cast ibex standing on a plinth projects from the front of the burner and may have functioned as a handle. The object is associated with the importance of incense in the religion of southwestern Arabia.",196;;;;;;;

196,0-آثارنا المنهوبة 30_p020_5b4e7eb9,"Standing bull ca. mid- to late 1st millennium BCE Not on view This bronze bull stands on a double-based plinth. Its head is strongly sculpted with upright ears behind horns that curve up and slightly inward. Heavy brows arch over the eyes, which are inlaid with shell. The tail hangs straight down to the hooves and parallels those of southwestern Arabian statues of standing bulls sculpted in alabaster, suggesting that this piece dates to the late first millennium B.C. like the alabaster bulls. By the middle of the first millennium B.C., kingdoms had emerged in southwestern Arabia whose power was based on control of the trade in frankincense and myrrh, which are native to the region. The kingdoms' immense wealth is reflected in the use of bronze to cast large sculptures, as well as smaller objects, which were produced throughout most of the first millennium B.C. Bulls are commonly represented and can be found on funerary stelae, seals, and sculpture. 197,0-آثارنا المنهوبة 30_p021_65971c7e,"Figure of ibex ca. 5th century BCE Not on view This bronze ibex stands on a base. Its distinctive long horns turn back to touch the upright ears. The ibex was the most widely represented animal in southwestern Arabian art. Ritual ibex hunts were an important feature of the cult practices of the southwestern Arabian kingdoms. Successfully capturing and killing these elusive creatures was believed to secure favors from the gods. This sculpture may have served as a handle for an incense burner similar to one in the Museum’s collection (MMA 49.71.2). From the middle of the first millennium B.C. until the sixth century A.D., the kingdoms of southwestern Arabia gained considerable wealth and power through their control of the trade in incense between Arabia and the lands of the Mediterranean seacoast. Frankincense and myrrh, gum resins that are native to southern Arabia, were widely valued in the ancient world for the preparation of incense, perfumes, cosmetics, and medicines, as well as for use in religious and funerary ceremonies. The city of Marib was also in an ideal position to control the trade route between India to Egypt, although this lucrative venture was cut short in the 1st century AD following the foundation of a nautical route from India directly to the port of Alexandria. Detailing is limited, with incised eyebrows and a single line at the apex of the forehead that seems designed to suggest a headband or similar. Read more This striking head is a grave marker or perhaps a commemorative piece from the ancient kingdom of Saba which ruled over the lands of southwestern Arabia, centered in modern day Yemen. The wealth of the kingdom is legendary, and is primarily attributable to Saba’s position at the crossroads of the ancient world, receiving consignments (particularly of incense) from all across the Middle East, Asia and the Mediterranean basin. Technically, the Sabeans were one of four major powers in this area, also including the Minaeans, the Qatabanians and the Hadramites, but the peoples as a whole became subsumed as a single entity by the middle of the first millennium BC. This piece depicts a feminine head and neck, and appears to have once served as a grave marker, or perhaps as part of a larger alabaster structure. Their religion and mythology fuelled the themes of their sculptural works – primarily anthropomorphic and zoomorphic statuary – while their contact with other cultures and nations led to a highly derived and distinctive style.",593,,,"Bust, alabaster. Ask about this lot Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents << Previous Lot Next Lot >> View Details At the beginning of each ""Ner"" (name of chapter in the book Menorat HaMaor) is a red and black ornamentation [with the exception of the sixth and seventh Ner]. Menorat HaMaor was embraced by Yemenite Jews and their communities adopted the custom of reading this composition in their synagogues every Shabbat afternoon. This is a complete manuscript of the composition (with the exception of a number of index leaves missing at the end), written before this well-known printing. Title page illustrated with a frame (in two colors): ""The book Menorat HaMaor was written by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav the Sephardi…written here in Sana'a…1848"". In 1857, Rabbi Moshe Chanoch HaLevi, a Jew from Aden who reached Italy, printed an edition of Menorat HaMaor in Livorno for his Yemenite brethren. For this purpose, the book was divided into chapters for Shabbat and festival days throughout the year.",597,,,"Manuscript on paper, Menorat HaMaor by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav, written in Sana'a, Yemen, 1848. Complete manuscript with the exception of several index leaves missing at the end, decorated with red and black ornamentation at the beginning of each chapter, except the sixth and seventh chapters.","Manuscript on paper, Menorat HaMaor by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav, written in Sana'a, Yemen, 1848. The manuscript is described as complete, apart from a number of index leaves missing at the end. At the beginning of each Ner, or chapter, there is red and black ornamentation, with the exception of the sixth and seventh Ners. The title page is illustrated with a two-colour frame and states that the book Menorat HaMaor was written by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav the Sephardi and written here in Sana'a in 1848. The text notes that Menorat HaMaor was widely embraced by Yemenite Jews and read in synagogues on Shabbat afternoon.",203;;;;;;;

203,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_22_p010_7fb500f3,"picture Auction 51 Part I - Books Chassidism Manuscripts Rabbinical Letters July 11, 2016 PDF Results Prices Realized Browse Sale << Previous Lot Lot 215 Next Lot >> Manuscript, Mipi Chachamim - Yemen, 1895 Opening: $500 Sold for: $875 Including buyer's premium Manuscript, Mipi Chachamim, halachic, aggadic and kabbalistic explanations for various laws and customs. Ask about this lot Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents << Previous Lot Next Lot >> View Details This manuscript has many variations from the printed version and corresponds to another manuscript written nine months earlier by the same scribe Rabbi Oded son of Yafet in the month of Iyar 1894, ""by the desire of my master, Zecharya"". · Long letter to ""My master and crown of my head"" Rabbi Salam son of Menachem, by ""Yuda Nissim, and Chassan Yuda, and Machbuv Avni"". Interesting letter related to Aliya to Eretz Israel and about their travails ""that G-d confronted us with a foe like Haman and even worse than Haman, his name and memory should be erased…"". Colophon of the scribe Rabbi Oded son of Yafet [Ra'avad of Aden, Ibb district, Southern Yemen] who wrote it ""by the desire of Chassan Utz and his sons"". [133] leaves, 25 cm + 9 leaves of varied size. On the endpapers and on additional leaves at the beginning and end are ownership inscriptions and various lists: · Prayers.",598,,,"Manuscript, paper, Mipi Chachamim, containing halachic, aggadic, and kabbalistic explanations of various laws and customs, Yemen, 1895.","Manuscript, paper, Mipi Chachamim, with halachic, aggadic, and kabbalistic explanations for various laws and customs. The manuscript is described as having many variations from the printed version and as corresponding to another manuscript written nine months earlier by the same scribe, Rabbi Oded son of Yafet, in Iyar 1894, by the desire of his master, Zecharya. It includes a long letter addressed to Rabbi Salam son of Menachem by Yuda Nissim, Chassan Yuda, and Machbuv Avni, concerning aliyah to Eretz Israel and their travails, and contains a colophon by the scribe Rabbi Oded son of Yafet, identified as Ra'avad of Aden, Ibb district, Southern Yemen, stating that he wrote it by the desire of Chassan Utz and his sons. The manuscript comprises 133 leaves, with an additional 9 leaves of varied size, measures 25 cm, and has ownership inscriptions and various lists, including prayers, on the endpapers and on additional leaves at the beginning and end.",204;;;;;;;

column1: 205,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_22_p012_ec9c65d5,"picture Auction 51 Part I - Books Chassidism Manuscripts Rabbinical Letters July 11, 2016 PDF Results Prices Realized Browse Sale << Previous Lot Lot 218 Next Lot >> Two Manuscripts, Prayers and Piyyutim - Yemen, 19th Century Opening: $450 Sold for: $625 Including buyer's premium Two Yemenite manuscripts, with original leather bindings. Ask about this lot Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents << Previous Lot Next Lot >> View Details Piyyutim of Bakashot, Tikun Chatzot, interpretation of dreams, tekufot and amulets, blessings and versions of shtarot, Birkat HaMazon with ""Chiddushin"", eulogies and a compilation of commentaries. Provenance: Collection of Willy Lindwer. Ancient leather binding with leather loops and adornments - worn, with worming. Scribe's colophon: ""Ya'ish son of Yichye Al Tza'adi"" from 1864. Ancient leather binding, with folk ornamentation, worn. Prayers, piyyutim of Selichot and Hosha'anot.",600,625,USD,"Manuscript, paper, with original leather binding. Yemen, 19th century. Two Yemenite manuscripts containing prayers and piyyutim, including Bakashot, Tikun Chatzot, dream interpretation, tekufot and amulets, blessings and versions of shtarot, Birkat HaMazon with ""Chiddushin"", eulogies, and a compilation of commentaries. The binding is worn and shows worming.","Manuscript, paper, with original leather binding. Yemen, 19th century. Two Yemenite manuscripts with original leather bindings, containing piyyutim of Bakashot, Tikun Chatzot, interpretation of dreams, tekufot and amulets, blessings and versions of shtarot, Birkat HaMazon with ""Chiddushin"", eulogies, and a compilation of commentaries. The manuscripts also include prayers and piyyutim of Selichot and Hosha'anot. One scribe's colophon names ""Ya'ish son of Yichye Al Tza'adi"" and is dated 1864. The bindings are described as ancient leather bindings with leather loops and adornments, worn and wormed, with folk ornamentation. Provenance: Collection of Willy Lindwer.",206;;;;;;;

206,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_22_p014_f8a5f56a,"picture Auction 51 Part I - Books Chassidism Manuscripts Rabbinical Letters July 11, 2016 PDF Results Prices Realized Browse Sale << Previous Lot Lot 222 Next Lot >> Manuscript - Siddur for Year-round Use - Yemen, 19th Century Opening: $600 Sold for: $813 Including buyer's premium Manuscript, siddur for year-round use - for weekdays, Shabbat and festivals. Ask about this lot Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents << Previous Lot Next Lot >> View Details Contains prayers for weekdays and for Shabbat, prayers for Rosh Chodesh, Birkat HaLevana, Seder for Chanuka, Tikun Purim, Tikun Pesach with the Haggadah, prayers for the Three Festivals and Azharot for Shavuot. On verso of title page: ""Version of blessings and prayers, according to our tradition of the holy congregation throughout Yemenite cities"". Title page with illustrated frame: ""Siddur of prayers according to Sephardic tradition, and written for the entire year…"". The last 20 leaves (Approximately) [prayers for the Festival of Shavuot and Azharot] are in a different handwriting. Stains and wear, tears [affecting the text of several leaves]. Some leaves at the end were replaced in another handwriting.",602,,,"Manuscript, siddur on paper for year-round use, Yemen, 19th century. Contains prayers for weekdays, Shabbat, festivals, Rosh Chodesh, Birkat HaLevana, Seder for Chanuka, Tikun Purim, Tikun Pesach with the Haggadah, the Three Festivals, and Azharot for Shavuot.","Manuscript, siddur on paper for year-round use, Yemen, 19th century. The manuscript includes prayers for weekdays and Shabbat, prayers for Rosh Chodesh, Birkat HaLevana, Seder for Chanuka, Tikun Purim, Tikun Pesach with the Haggadah, prayers for the Three Festivals, and Azharot for Shavuot. The verso of the title page bears the note: ""Version of blessings and prayers, according to our tradition of the holy congregation throughout Yemenite cities"". The title page has an illustrated frame and is inscribed: ""Siddur of prayers according to Sephardic tradition, and written for the entire year…"". The last approximately 20 leaves, containing prayers for the Festival of Shavuot and Azharot, are in a different handwriting, and some leaves at the end were replaced in another handwriting. The manuscript shows stains, wear, and tears affecting the text of several leaves.",207;;;;;;;

207,0-آثارنا المنهوبة_22_p015_88c123d1,"picture Auction 51 Part I - Books Chassidism Manuscripts Rabbinical Letters July 11, 2016 PDF Results Prices Realized Browse Sale << Previous Lot Lot 224 Next Lot >> Three Manuscripts - Yemen Opening: $400 Sold for: $1,000 Including buyer's premium Three small volumes of manuscripts, with compilations of various topics. Ask about this lot Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents Yemenite Jewry - Manuscripts and Documents << Previous Lot Next Lot >> View Details Manuscript, compilation by various writers and from various times: story of Jesus, leap years, death of Aharon the Cohen, oaths, te'amim according to Kabbalah, etc. Manuscript, compilation by various writers and from various times: laws of shechita, Shabbat songs, te'amim, etc. Part of the Passover Haggadah appears in the beginning of the manuscript [in another handwriting]. Three volumes of manuscripts, approximately 200 leaves. Manuscript, Birkat Erusin, piyyutim, bakashot.",603,1000,USD,"Manuscript, paper, three small volumes of manuscripts from Yemen, comprising compilations by various writers and from various times on diverse topics, including the story of Jesus, leap years, the death of Aharon the Cohen, oaths, te'amim according to Kabbalah, laws of shechita, Shabbat songs, part of the Passover Haggadah, Birkat Erusin, piyyutim, and bakashot.","Manuscript, paper, three small volumes of manuscripts from Yemen, compiled by various writers and from various times. The contents include the story of Jesus, leap years, the death of Aharon the Cohen, oaths, te'amim according to Kabbalah, laws of shechita, Shabbat songs, and a section of the Passover Haggadah in another hand at the beginning of one manuscript. The group comprises approximately 200 leaves in total. One manuscript also contains Birkat Erusin, piyyutim, and bakashot.",208;;;;;;;

The figure wears a long dress and holds a rectangular hollowed-out votive bowl.","Female votive figure, marble with an applied gesso layer painted with coloured details, standing frontally on a small rectangular integral plinth and dated to circa 1st century B.C. The figure wears a long dress, waisted and ending at the calves above ankle bracelets, with incised linear and cross-hatched ornament over the legs and around the upper arms. She is shown with strands of necklaces composed of triangular pendants and a large central crescentic pendant above the breasts, and her arms are bent at the elbows with the forearms projecting forward to hold a rectangular hollowed-out votive bowl with incised panels. The oval face has a pointed chin, strong browline, incised arched brows, large eyes with drilled pupils, a straight nose, and a small mouth. The hair flares below the ears and falls to the shoulders, with incised linear banded details and a diadem of circular pendants above the forehead. Height: 31 in (79 cm).",215;;;;;;;

218,29-آثارنا المنهوبة_29_p019_5186eca2,"Bull Protome Qataban Kingdom, Yemen 700-100 BC Bronze Height : 18 cm Provenance : Sotheby's New York, 1999, lot 84 Galerie David Ghezelbash, Paris (acquired from above) Private collection, Paris Publication : Kunstkammer, 2017, n°3 In the shape of the powerful forequarters of a bull, his massive head with round muzzle, grooved nostrils, and ribbed bulging brows, symbolic motifs and a crescent moon engraved on the forehead, a lozenge-decorated diadem between the horns. The national god of each of the kingdoms or states was the Moon-god known by various names: ‘Ilumquh by the Sabaeans, ‘Amm and ‘Anbay by the Qatabanians, Wadd (love) by the Minaeans, and Sin by the Hadramis”. Amongst the South Arabians the worship of the moon continued, and it is almost certain that their religious calendar was also lunar and that their years were calculated by the position of the moon. This rare Sabean inscription is dedicated to the moon god and refers to the people of Qataban, the most prominent Yemeni kingdom in the second half of the 1st millenium before Christ. This, combined with the use of a star calendar by the agriculturists of certain parts, particularly in the Hadramaut, indicates that there was an early reverence for the night sky. Muller, from the Center for Near and Middle-Eastern studies of Philipps Univeristy of Marburg, describes in detail the context and the meaning of these different motifs. “The South Arabians before Islam were polytheists and revered a large number of deities. It was essentially a planetary system in which the moon as a masculine deity prevailed.",619,,,"Bull protome, bronze, Qataban Kingdom, Yemen, 700-100 BC. Height 18 cm. The object is formed as the powerful forequarters of a bull, with a massive head, round muzzle, grooved nostrils, ribbed bulging brows, engraved symbolic motifs, a crescent moon on the forehead, and a lozenge-decorated diadem between the horns.","Bull protome, bronze, Qataban Kingdom, Yemen, 700-100 BC. Height 18 cm. The object is shaped as the powerful forequarters of a bull, with a massive head, round muzzle, grooved nostrils, and ribbed bulging brows. The forehead is engraved with symbolic motifs and a crescent moon, and a lozenge-decorated diadem is placed between the horns. The source text identifies it as a rare Sabean inscription dedicated to the moon god and referring to the people of Qataban, the most prominent Yemeni kingdom in the second half of the 1st millennium BC.",219;;;;;;;

219,29-آثارنا المنهوبة_29_p021_11b31839,"Since almost all warriors from the rims of vessels as well as figures in appliqué on the shoulders of craters are shown moving from left to right, our figure probably had a counterpart facing him on the edge of a vessel or belonged to a free-standing group. If there was trade with Arabia in incense, myrrh and other spices in the 6th century B.C., it would have been conducted by East Greeks, but the figure might easily have passed from hand to hand via Egypt or the Levant. Published: Beazley, J.D.: An Archaic Greek Statuette from South Arabia, BSA 40, 1939-40 (publ. The double moulding on the left side of the helmet crest seems not to have been repeated on the right side, if this be so, the figure is to be seen from the left. - Jucker, H.: Bronzehenkel und Bronzehydria aus Pesaro, StOliv 13-14, 1966, p. Warrior - Archaic 47/103 Down Bronze H: 17 cm From Yemen Laconian? M.: Statuettes de bronze de Lykosoura, BCH 99, 1975, no. 1 Berlin, Antikenmuseum 7470: Neugebauer, K.-A.: Die griechischen Bronzen der klassischen Zeit und des Hellenismus (Berlin, 1951), no.",620,,,"Bronze statuette of a warrior, Archaic period, 17 cm high, from Yemen. The figure is described as facing left, with a helmet crest showing double moulding on the left side only.","Bronze statuette of a warrior, Archaic period, 17 cm high, from Yemen. The figure is identified as a warrior and is described as being seen from the left, since the double moulding on the left side of the helmet crest does not appear to have been repeated on the right side. It is suggested that the figure may originally have belonged to a larger composition, either as a counterpart to another figure on the rim of a vessel or as part of a free-standing group. The object has been discussed in relation to Laconian bronzes and published by Beazley and Jucker.",220;;;;;;;

220,29-آثارنا المنهوبة_29_p043_255b10d7,"Form Through Time Auction: 31 May 2023 at 15:00 BST £10,080 Description carved striped alabaster, the slender neck rising to a rounded chin, the face bearing a compact mouth, the upper lip topped with a small moustache, the ridge nose parting two almond shaped eyes below a sweeping incised brow, raised on a bespoke mount Dimensions 20.9cm tall (excluding stand) Provenance Provenance: Previously in the Private Collection of Mr François Antonovich (b.
